stem: Menopause is formally defined as a retrospective diagnosis. Which criterion PRECISELY establishes this diagnosis?
options: Absence of menstruation for 6 consecutive months with elevated FSH levels confirmed on two separate occasions
Absence of menstruation for 12 consecutive months representing the last menstrual period
Absence of menstruation for 24 consecutive months in the absence of other pathological causes
Cessation of menstruation for 6 months accompanied by confirmed bilateral ovarian failure on ultrasound

stem: The age range within which natural menopause is MOST expected to occur in females is:
options: 40 to 50 years
42 to 52 years
45 to 55 years
48 to 58 years

stem: With advancing age at menopause, ovarian follicles begin to atrophy and ovarian oestradiol levels fall to very low values. This decline removes negative feedback inhibition from which two structures, resulting in gross elevation of gonadotropins?
options: Anterior pituitary and adrenal cortex
Hypothalamus and anterior pituitary
Hypothalamus and posterior pituitary
Pineal gland and hypothalamus

stem: Following the cessation of ovarian follicular function at menopause, the predominant circulating oestrogen shifts. Which oestrogen is predominant in the post-menopausal state, and what is its PRIMARY source?
options: Oestradiol, produced predominantly by the adrenal medulla via direct secretion
Oestrone, produced by adrenal and ovarian stromal cells via peripheral aromatization of androgens
Oestriol, produced primarily by hepatic conversion of oestradiol in a first-pass effect
Oestradiol, produced by the corpus luteum persisting from the final ovarian cycle

stem: The term 'climacteric' has a specific and distinct meaning from the term 'menopause.' Which definition MOST precisely captures the climacteric?
options: The single calendar date of the last menstrual period confirmed after 12 months of amenorrhea
The period of waning ovarian function that encompasses both the menopause and the post-menopausal phase
The 12-month transitional period immediately preceding the last menstrual period only
The period of complete gonadal inactivity commencing exactly 5 years after the last menstrual period

stem: Menstrual changes at the climacteric follow a predictable initial pattern before permanent cessation occurs. Which statement MOST accurately describes the earliest menstrual pattern change?
options: Menstruation immediately and abruptly ceases without any preceding change in cycle regularity
Menstruation becomes scanty and infrequent before abruptly stopping
Menstruation initially becomes irregular and heavy before eventually stopping permanently
Menstruation transitions to a regular lighter flow lasting 2 to 3 days before final cessation

stem: Which of the following psychological symptoms is specifically listed among the changes occurring at menopause as a direct result of hormonal alteration?
options: Euphoria, hypersomnia, and reduced appetite
Depression, insomnia, irritability, nervousness, and headaches
Psychosis, visual hallucinations, and profound memory impairment
Anxiety disorder, panic attacks, and obsessive-compulsive traits

stem: Menopausal vasomotor changes produce a characteristic and well-recognized symptom. Which vasomotor symptom is SPECIFICALLY mentioned in this clinical context?
options: Excessive perspiration during sleep (night sweats) without accompanying skin temperature changes
Hot flushes
Episodic palpitations with concurrent systolic hypertension lasting more than 30 minutes
Raynaud's phenomenon causing digital colour changes on exposure to temperature variation

stem: Physical changes at menopause include genital atrophy resulting from collagen loss. The clinical symptom MOST directly caused by this genital atrophy in post-menopausal women is:
options: Urge urinary incontinence due to detrusor muscle hyperactivity
Dyspareunia due to the atrophic changes affecting the vaginal epithelium and lubrication
Pelvic organ prolapse due to levator ani muscle atrophy and fascial weakness
Endometrial hyperplasia due to unopposed estrogen stimulation of the atrophic endometrium

stem: The metabolic consequences of menopause increase the risk of coronary artery disease through a specific lipid profile change. Which lipid alteration is CHARACTERISTIC of this menopausal metabolic shift?
options: High HDL and low LDL resulting in paradoxically increased atherosclerotic risk
High LDL and low HDL resulting in an atherogenic lipid profile
High triglycerides with proportionally low LDL and normal HDL levels
Uniformly elevated total cholesterol with no change in HDL to LDL ratio

stem: Premature menopause is defined by which specific criterion regarding the age of menstruation cessation?
options: Cessation of menstruation before 45 years of age
Cessation of menstruation before 40 years of age
Cessation of menstruation before 35 years of age
Cessation of menstruation before 42 years of age

stem: Although the testicular function gradually declines with age in males, the male equivalent of the female menopause does NOT occur. Which statement MOST accurately explains why the male lacks a complete equivalent of menopause?
options: Male testosterone levels decline at the same absolute rate as female oestradiol but the threshold for infertility is never reached
The function of the testis declines slowly with age but the ability to produce spermatozoa persists and may never completely cease
The hypothalamic-pituitary axis in the male undergoes compensatory upregulation preventing the gonadotropin elevation seen in females
Male sex hormone production is sustained by adrenal compensation once testicular function begins to decline at approximately age 50

stem: Hormone replacement therapy (HRT) is primarily indicated when target hormone levels are low or absent. During which specific age range of a woman's reproductive life is HRT most commonly indicated according to the content of this curriculum?
options: Ages 13 to 44 years
Ages 15 to 44 years
Ages 18 to 44 years
Ages 20 to 44 years

stem: Among the following estrogen formulations specifically listed as constituting post-menopausal replacement therapy, which combination is MOST complete?
options: 17 beta-estradiol, synthetic ethinyl estradiol, and estriol only
17 beta-estradiol, conjugated equine estrogens, esterified estrogens, and estriol
Conjugated equine estrogens, estriol, and diethylstilboestrol only
17 beta-estradiol, esterified estrogens, and medroxyprogesterone acetate

stem: Non-oral routes of HRT estrogen delivery are specifically mentioned. Which non-oral delivery systems are named in this curriculum?
options: Subcutaneous implants, nasal sprays, and transdermal creams exclusively
Matrix patches, reservoir patches, gel, and intravaginal administration
Sublingual tablets, vaginal rings, and intrauterine systems only
Intramuscular injections, suppositories, and transdermal aerosols

stem: Naturally occurring progestogens are used in HRT formulations. Which specific naturally occurring progestogen compounds are named in this curriculum?
options: Medroxyprogesterone acetate (MPA) and norethisterone acetate
Progesterone P4 and 17-alpha OH progesterone
Dydrogesterone and micronised progesterone only
Levonorgestrel and norgestimate as naturally derived compounds

stem: HRT regimens are given in a manner that best approximates normal physiology. In what specific manner are the components administered?
options: Simultaneously as a fixed daily combination dose to ensure constant hormonal levels
Sequentially to mimic the natural cycle
Cyclically with a monthly hormone-free interval to allow endometrial shedding
Continuously without interruption to prevent any withdrawal bleeding permanently

stem: A 52-year-old post-menopausal woman requests HRT. Her clinician reviews her history for absolute contraindications. Which finding would constitute an ABSOLUTE contraindication to HRT?
options: A history of migraine headaches without aura managed with triptans
Personal history of breast cancer treated 8 years ago
Active coronary artery disease (CAD)
Atypical ductal hyperplasia of the breast diagnosed on biopsy 3 years ago

stem: For HRT, undiagnosed vaginal bleeding is listed as an absolute contraindication. Which statement BEST explains the clinical rationale for this absolute contraindication?
options: HRT estrogen will exacerbate the bleeding by promoting endometrial hyperplasia before the underlying cause is identified
The bleeding may originate from a source such as endometrial malignancy that could be masked or stimulated by exogenous hormones before diagnosis
Progesterone in the HRT regimen will suppress ovulation and aggravate any corpus luteum deficiency causing the bleeding
HRT will cause immediate coagulation pathway disruption, converting light spotting into life-threatening haemorrhage

stem: Well-differentiated and early endometrial cancer is listed as an absolute contraindication to HRT with a notable exception. What is this exception?
options: Once the patient has been cancer-free for 2 years, HRT is no longer absolutely contraindicated
Once treatment for the malignancy is complete, it is no longer an absolute contraindication; progestins alone may relieve symptoms if estrogen cannot be tolerated
Once the patient demonstrates a high progesterone-to-estrogen ratio, HRT may be cautiously resumed
Once the histological grade has been re-classified as benign on repeat sampling, HRT may be initiated

stem: CASE STUDY: A 55-year-old woman presents with hot flushes, insomnia, irritability, and a new-onset aching pain during intercourse over the past 8 months. Her last menstrual period was 14 months ago. She has a BMI of 24 and no prior medical history. She asks for treatment. Before initiating HRT, the nurse checks for absolute contraindications. Which finding in her history, if present, would MOST definitively preclude HRT?
options: Her mother having had breast cancer at age 72 years
A diagnosis of uterine fibroids 6 years ago that were managed conservatively
A current DVT in the right popliteal vein diagnosed 3 weeks ago
A history of cholecystitis that was surgically resolved 4 years ago

stem: CASE STUDY: The same 55-year-old woman from Q21 denies all absolute contraindications. Her assessment identifies only migraine headaches and a personal history of ovarian cancer. The nurse correctly categorises these findings under which classification in relation to HRT?
options: Absolute contraindications mandating permanent exclusion from HRT
Relative contraindications that require careful risk-benefit assessment before initiating HRT
Incidental findings with no bearing whatsoever on the safety of initiating HRT
Indications that favour the use of progestins alone in place of combined HRT

stem: CASE STUDY: The 55-year-old woman from Q21 is initiated on HRT. After 3 weeks she calls the clinic reporting breast tenderness and ankle swelling. The nurse identifies these as recognized side effects of HRT. Which mechanism MOST directly explains the ankle swelling reported?
options: Progesterone-mediated arterial vasodilation reducing venous return from the lower extremities
Fluid retention secondary to the sodium and water-retaining effects of the hormonal components of HRT
Allergic vasculitis triggered by the synthetic excipients contained in the oral HRT formulation
Venous thrombosis formation in the deep veins of the calf as an early HRT complication

stem: CASE STUDY: A 49-year-old woman being evaluated for HRT has a history of atypical ductal hyperplasia of the breast confirmed on biopsy. The nurse is preparing her pre-HRT counselling. Under which specific contraindication category does atypical ductal hyperplasia of the breast fall?
options: It is an absolute contraindication identical in strength to active endometrial cancer
It is a relative contraindication requiring careful clinical consideration before initiating HRT
It is not a contraindication because it is a pre-malignant rather than a malignant lesion
It is a relative contraindication only if accompanied by a concurrent personal history of estrogen-receptor positive breast cancer

stem: CASE STUDY: A 46-year-old woman with a history of active gallbladder cholangitis and cholecystitis requests HRT for severe menopausal symptoms. The nurse correctly classifies her gallbladder disease under which category of HRT contraindication?
options: Absolute contraindication â active gallbladder disease is listed among absolute contraindications to HRT
Relative contraindication â active gallbladder disease including cholangitis and cholecystitis is listed as a relative contraindication
Not a contraindication â gallbladder disease has no established interaction with HRT hormone components
Absolute contraindication only if bilirubin levels are greater than twice the upper limit of normal

stem: CASE STUDY: A male patient aged 16 years was castrated before the onset of puberty due to a medical emergency involving testicular torsion with bilateral infarction. During a follow-up consultation at age 22, which physical profile is MOST expected?
options: Deep voice, fully developed muscular build, and normal-sized genitals consistent with complete pubertal development
High-pitched voice, non-muscular build, small genitals, and possibly taller than average stature
High-pitched voice, muscular build from growth hormone compensation, and normal-sized genitals
Normal voice pitch, small genitals, and short stature due to early epiphyseal closure from preserved adrenal androgens

stem: CASE STUDY: The patient from Q26 who was castrated before puberty is taller than average for his age group. The nurse explains the mechanism of this height advantage. Which physiological explanation is MOST accurate?
options: Absence of testosterone prevents the pre-pubertal growth hormone surge, causing continuous compensatory growth hormone secretion throughout adolescence
Reduction in sex hormone production reduces oestrogen (derived via aromatization of testosterone), which delays or prevents early epiphyseal closure permitting continued bone length growth
Absence of testicular androgens triggers hypothalamic upregulation of IGF-1, which is the sole determinant of the increased height
Loss of negative feedback from testosterone leads to gross elevation of LH, which independently stimulates long bone growth plates via a direct receptor mechanism

stem: CASE STUDY: The same pre-pubertal castration patient (Q26) asks whether pubic hair ever develops after castration before puberty. Which response is clinically MOST accurate based on the curriculum?
options: Pubic hair will never develop under any circumstances as all androgenic stimulus has been permanently removed
Pubic hair may develop (driven by adrenal androgens) but libido will be reduced or absent entirely
Pubic hair will develop fully because growth hormone independently drives adrenarche regardless of testicular status
Pubic hair and libido will develop normally because peripheral tissues convert adrenal cortisol to active androgens

stem: When castration occurs AFTER the onset of puberty, which effect on libido is MOST expected compared to pre-pubertal castration?
options: Libido is completely unaffected because the hypothalamic set-point for sexual drive was established during puberty
Castration after the onset of puberty will typically substantially reduce or completely eliminate libido
Libido increases after post-pubertal castration due to a rebound in LH-driven adrenal androgen production
Libido is transiently reduced for 6 to 12 months before normalizing as adrenal androgens compensate fully

stem: Castration at any age renders the individual permanently sterile. Which mechanism MOST precisely explains the basis of post-castration sterility?
options: Castrated individuals become sterile because the pituitary ceases to produce FSH without gonadal negative feedback
Castrated individuals cannot produce eggs or sperm as the gonads, which are the sole sites of gametogenesis, have been removed
Castration abolishes ejaculation reflexes at the spinal cord level, preventing gamete transport even if germ cells survived
The vas deferens fibroses after castration, creating mechanical obstruction regardless of residual spermatogenic tissue

stem: Sex hormones are thought to prime the action of growth hormone before puberty, enhancing the secondary growth spurt. Where in the pubertal timeline does this secondary growth spurt typically appear?
options: At the precise midpoint of the pubertal timeline when gonadotropin levels peak
Just before the onset of puberty
Concurrently with the first appearance of secondary sexual characteristics
After the completion of all secondary sexual characteristics including genital maturation

stem: The curriculum states that sudden castration at the point just before pubertal onset produces a specific skeletal effect that is distinct from the effect of castration done well before puberty. What is this distinct effect?
options: It will accelerate general skeletal growth beyond the normal pubertal growth spurt amplitude
It will not affect general skeletal growth but will inhibit early epiphyseal closure
It will immediately halt all longitudinal bone growth by depriving growth plates of androgen co-stimulation
It will produce equivalent height outcomes to pre-pubertal castration because epiphyseal closure has not yet begun

stem: Precocious puberty is said to occur when secondary sexual characteristics develop before a defined age threshold. What age range defines precocious puberty?
options: Before 6 to 8 years
Before 7 to 9 years
Before 8 to 10 years
Before 9 to 11 years

stem: Among the causes of precocious puberty, which aetiology accounts for the MAJORITY of cases in the gonadotropin-dependent category?
options: Hypothalamic hamartoma producing pulsatile GnRH
Idiopathic (unknown cause) in the majority of cases
Langerhans cell histiocytosis involving the hypothalamus
Tumour in the hypothalamo-pituitary axis

stem: Among the causes of gonadotropin-dependent precocious puberty, a specific hypothalamic lesion can independently produce pulsatile GnRH release, triggering the HPO axis prematurely. This lesion is:
options: Craniopharyngioma located in the suprasellar region
Hypothalamic hamartoma
Langerhans cell histiocytosis of the hypothalamic neurons
Pinealoma causing local hypothalamic compression

stem: Gonadotropin-independent precocious puberty can result from fetal or postnatal exogenous hormonal exposure. Which exogenous hormone source is specifically named as a cause in this curriculum?
options: Maternal use of thyroid replacement therapy during pregnancy
Exposure to oestrogens or androgens from therapeutic administration or tumour-producing hormones
Exogenous growth hormone therapy administered in early infancy
Maternal use of progestin-only contraception during the first trimester

stem: McCune-Albright syndrome is specifically listed among the causes of precocious puberty. Under which pathophysiological category of precocious puberty does this syndrome fall?
options: Gonadotropin-dependent (central) precocious puberty via hypothalamic activation
Gonadotropin-independent precocious puberty via autonomous peripheral gonadal activity
Eugonadotrophic delayed puberty with paradoxical early virilization
Hypergonadotropic hypogonadism with intermittent autonomous steroid secretion

stem: The three therapeutic goals of treating precocious puberty in a girl-child are specifically stated in this curriculum. Which combination CORRECTLY identifies all three goals?
options: Induce normal puberty at an appropriate age, prevent early fertility, and maximize adult bone mineral density
Protect the girl-child from sexual abuse, suppress premature activation of the HPO axis, and prevent short stature
Normalize gonadotropin levels, prevent early menarche, and promote a normal body image
Prevent epiphyseal closure, induce regression of secondary sexual characteristics, and normalize adrenal androgen levels

stem: Management of precocious puberty requires specific investigations. According to the curriculum, which imaging modalities and laboratory assessments are CRITICAL in the diagnostic workup?
options: DEXA scan for bone age, thyroid function tests, and fasting insulin levels
X-rays, ultrasound, CT, and MRI as well as reproductive hormonal assays
Karyotyping, adrenal vein sampling, and laparoscopy for gonadal biopsy
EEG, skull X-ray, and 24-hour urinary catecholamine assay

stem: Delayed puberty is defined by two specific clinical scenarios. Which combination CORRECTLY captures BOTH diagnostic criteria for delayed puberty?
options: Secondary sexual characteristics absent at 18 years in males OR menarche absent at 16 years in females
Secondary sexual characteristics absent at 20 years in males and menarche absent at 17 years in females, OR failure of mensuration five years after commencement of puberty
No breast development at 14 years in females OR no testicular enlargement at 14 years in males
Secondary sexual characteristics absent at 16 years in males and menarche absent at 15 years in females

stem: Delayed puberty caused by hypergonadotropic hypogonadism is characterised by which specific gonadotropin pattern and its most common underlying aetiology?
options: Low FSH and LH due to hypothalamic GnRH deficiency from a tumour or congenital cause
High FSH and LH due to primary ovarian failure (idiopathic, genetic, traumatic, iatrogenic, chemical, or radiation destruction)
Normal FSH and LH with end-organ (gonadal) resistance to circulating gonadotropins
Pulsatile FSH with tonic LH elevation due to autonomous adrenal androgen overproduction

stem: Hypogonadotropic hypogonadism as a cause of delayed puberty is distinguished from hypergonadotropic hypogonadism by which gonadotropin pattern, and which syndrome involves HP axis destruction causing this pattern?
options: High FSH/LH; Kallmann's syndrome and craniopharyngioma
Low FSH/LH; Kallmann's syndrome and craniopharyngioma as HP axis-destroying conditions
Low FSH/LH; McCune-Albright syndrome and congenital adrenal hyperplasia
Normal FSH/LH; isolated GnRH deficiency in Prader-Willi syndrome and constitutional delay

stem: Eugonadotrophic delayed puberty represents a third category of delayed puberty where gonadotropin levels are normal. Which specific anatomical obstructive conditions are named as causes in this category?
options: Bicornuate uterus, cervical stenosis, and endometrial adhesions (Asherman's syndrome)
Imperforate hymen, transverse vaginal septum, and androgen insensitivity
Ovarian agenesis, fallopian tube aplasia, and mÃ¼llerian agenesis
Polycystic ovarian syndrome, hypothyroidism, and hyperprolactinaemia

stem: Masters and Johnson conducted their pioneering research on human sexual response by direct observation of subjects. How many women and how many men participated in the initial phase of their studies from 1957 to 1965?
options: 300 women and 250 men
382 women and 312 men
400 women and 350 men
350 women and 300 men

stem: The research by Masters and Johnson from 1957 to 1965 conservatively estimated the total number of complete cycles of sexual response directly observed. What was this conservative estimate?
options: 5,000 complete cycles
8,000 complete cycles
10,000 complete cycles
12,000 complete cycles

stem: Masters and Johnson authored two landmark books based on their sexual response research. What were the publication years of 'Human Sexual Response' and 'Human Sexual Inadequacy' respectively?
Vulvo-vaginitis in children can result from multiple causes. Which combination of causes is MOST comprehensively listed in this curriculum?
options: Candida overgrowth, antibiotic use, synthetic underwear, and chlorinated pool water exposure
Low oestrogen, poor urinating and defecating, menstrual and social hygiene issues, foreign body insertion, trauma, and sexually transmitted diseases including sexual abuse
Group B streptococcal colonisation, viral infections, and lichen sclerosus in pre-pubertal children
Pinworm infestation, bubble bath irritants, nappy rash, and urinary tract contamination

stem: A child presenting with vulvo-vaginitis should be examined in which specific physical positions to allow adequate assessment?
options: Lithotomy position and left lateral (Sims) position
Knee-chest position and dorsal recumbent position
Knee-chest or frog-leg position
Standing squat position and left lateral decubitus position

stem: When examining a child with vulvo-vaginitis, what specific diagnostic action should the examining clinician always perform at the time of examination to maximize diagnostic yield?
options: Perform a colposcopy with acetic acid application to identify subclinical mucosal changes
Take a swab for culture and sensitivity
Perform a Papanicolaou smear to screen for viral cytopathic changes
Collect a mid-stream urine sample and blood for full septic workup simultaneously

stem: The primary reason why pre-pubertal girls are particularly predisposed to vulvo-vaginitis compared to post-pubertal females is MOST directly attributed to which physiological factor?
options: Absence of protective labial fat pads allowing direct environmental exposure of the vaginal introitus
Low oestrogen levels resulting in thin atrophic vaginal epithelium with absent glycogen-rich protective mucosal environment
Immature immune system incapable of producing secretory IgA for vaginal mucosal defence
Alkaline vaginal pH due to absent lactobacilli that only colonise the vagina after oestrogen-driven menarche

stem: Menopause is defined as a retrospective diagnosis made after waiting for ___ year/years without a period, representing the last menstrual period.
Natural menopause occurs between the ages of ___ and ___ years in females.

Although the function of the testis tends to decline slowly with advancing age, there is no male menopause similar to that obtained in the female because the ability to produce ___ persists and may never completely cease.

With advancing age, ovarian follicles begin to atrophy and ovarian hormones (specifically ___) continue to decline to very low levels, causing loss of feedback inhibition on the hypothalamus and pituitary, with eventual gross elevation of ___.
The predominant oestrogen in the post-menopausal state is ___, which is a product of adrenal and stromal cells of the ovary via peripheral ___ of androgens.
The term ___ refers to the period of waning of ovarian function that includes both the menopause and the post-menopausal period.
Premature menopause is defined as the cessation of menstruation before the age of ___ years.
Menopausal physical changes due to collagen loss result in genital atrophy, which causes ___ (painful intercourse), and osteoporosis which predisposes to ___.
The menopausal metabolic changes that increase coronary artery disease risk are characterized by a lipid profile of ___ LDL and ___ HDL.
Menopausal symptoms including hot flushes, genital atrophy, osteoporosis, and psychological changes can be reversed by hormone replacement therapy using ___ and ___.
HRT is used when target hormones are low or absent during the reproductive age of the woman, which is defined in this curriculum as ages ___ to ___ years.
The specific estrogen formulations that constitute postmenopausal HRT include 17 beta-estradiol, conjugated equine estrogens, esterified estrogens, and ___.
The two naturally occurring progestogens named in this curriculum for use in HRT formulations are Progesterone ___ and 17-___ OH progesterone.
In HRT, the progestogen and estrogen components are given ___ to mimic the natural cycle, rather than being administered simultaneously as a fixed combination.

The six absolute contraindications to HRT include: undiagnosed vaginal bleeding, severe liver disease, pregnancy, coronary artery disease, well-differentiated and early endometrial cancer, and recent ___ or ___.
Under the relative contraindications to HRT, the two cancer-related histories listed are personal history of ___ cancer and personal history of ___ cancer.
Active gallbladder disease is a ___ contraindication to HRT, and specifically includes the conditions ___ and ___.
Among the common side effects of HRT, swelling of the hands, feet, or lower legs occurs due to ___, while changes in the breast include tenderness, ___, or discharge.

A person castrated before the onset of puberty retains a ___ voice and a non-muscular build, and may be ___ than average because reduced oestrogen (from aromatization of testosterone) hinders early closure of epiphyses.
After castration before puberty, pubic hair ___ develop, but libido is reduced to ___ at all.
Castration performed after the onset of puberty typically ___ libido substantially or ___ it in total, and renders the individual automatically ___.
Precocious puberty is said to occur when secondary sexual characteristics appear before ___ to ___ years of age.

In the majority of cases, the cause of gonadotropin-dependent precocious puberty is ___, while a specific hypothalamic lesion called ___ hamartoma may produce pulsatile GnRH and trigger premature puberty.
Gonadotropin-independent precocious puberty can result from exogenous fetal or postnatal exposure to ___ or androgens, from therapeutic administration or ___ producing hormones.
The three goals of treating precocious puberty are: to protect the girl-child from ___, to suppress premature activation of the ___ axis, and to prevent ___ stature.
acceptableAnswers: sexual abuse / HPO axis / short stature

stem: Delayed puberty is defined as secondary sexual characteristics in males occurring after ___ years or menarche in females occurring after ___ years, OR failure of mensuration ___ years after commencement of puberty.

Hypergonadotropic hypogonadism as a cause of delayed puberty is characterised by ___ FSH/LH levels due to primary ovarian failure, whereas hypogonadotropic hypogonadism is characterised by ___ FSH/LH levels.
The two specific hypothalamo-pituitary axis-destroying conditions named in this curriculum that cause hypogonadotropic hypogonadism and thus delayed puberty are ___ syndrome and ___.

Eugonadotrophic delayed puberty occurs when gonadotropins are normal but physical obstruction prevents menstruation. Two anatomical causes listed in this category are imperforate ___ and transverse vaginal ___.
Masters and Johnson studied human sexual response from ___ until 1965, based on direct observation of ___ women and ___ men.
acceptableAnswers: nineteen fifty-seven / three hundred and eighty-two / three hundred and twelve

stem: Masters and Johnson published two classic texts on human sexuality: ___ published in 1966 and ___ published in 1970.
answer: Human Sexual Response / Human Sexual Inadequacy
acceptableAnswers: Human Sexual Response / Human Sexual Inadequacy
Human Sexual Response / Human Sexual Inadequacy (1970)

stem: Both of the classic texts by Masters and Johnson became best-sellers and were translated into more than ___ languages.
Vulvo-vaginitis in children may present with discharge, ___, pruritis, urinary symptoms, abnormal odour, or ___ of the vulva.

The primary reason pre-pubertal girls are more susceptible to vulvo-vaginitis is ___ oestrogen levels, which results in thin vaginal epithelium lacking the protective ___ rich mucosa seen after puberty.
Adolescent problems in gynaecology listed in this curriculum include oligomenorrhoea (DUB), menorrhagia, acute and chronic pelvic pain, ___ syndrome, and unwanted pregnancy.

CASE STUDY: A 7-year-old girl is brought to the clinic by her mother who noticed breast budding 2 weeks ago. The child also has axillary hair. There is no family history of early puberty. Serum LH and FSH levels are significantly elevated. Which category of precocious puberty does this presentation MOST likely represent?
options: Gonadotropin-independent precocious puberty caused by an autonomous ovarian cyst
Gonadotropin-dependent (central) precocious puberty with premature activation of the HPO axis
McCune-Albright syndrome causing gonadotropin-independent autonomous sex steroid production
Exogenous oestrogen exposure from a tumour-producing hormone in the adrenal gland

stem: CASE STUDY: The same 7-year-old girl with elevated gonadotropins is found on MRI to have a lesion in the hypothalamus producing pulsatile GnRH. This lesion is MOST likely:
options: A craniopharyngioma arising from Rathke's pouch remnants in the suprasellar region
A hypothalamic hamartoma
A Langerhans cell histiocytosis deposit within the hypothalamic nuclei
A pilocytic astrocytoma of the optic chiasm with secondary hypothalamic compression

stem: CASE STUDY: The parents of the 7-year-old with central precocious puberty ask what the long-term consequence of untreated precocious puberty would be on her height. The nurse explains the mechanism correctly as:
options: She will be significantly taller than average because early hormonal activation stimulates additional growth hormone pulses throughout puberty
She will ultimately be shorter than average because early sex hormone-driven epiphyseal closure will prematurely terminate her longitudinal bone growth
Her final adult height will be entirely unaffected because epiphyseal plates are not sensitive to sex hormones until after the normal pubertal age
She will have variable height outcomes depending solely on her genetic potential as measured by parental heights

stem: CASE STUDY: A 19-year-old female presents with primary amenorrhoea. She has no breast development and no pubic hair. Hormonal investigations reveal very high FSH and LH with very low oestradiol. The nurse correctly classifies this as which type of hypogonadism?
options: Hypogonadotropic hypogonadism with a central HPO axis defect causing low FSH and LH
Hypergonadotropic hypogonadism due to primary ovarian failure with no response to elevated gonadotropins
Eugonadotrophic hypogonadism with anatomical outflow obstruction at the level of the vagina
Hypogonadotropic hypogonadism secondary to excessive exercise and nutritional deficiency reducing hypothalamic GnRH

stem: CASE STUDY: For the same 19-year-old with hypergonadotropic hypogonadism (Q89), which specific aetiologies should FIRST be considered in the differential diagnosis based on the curriculum?
options: Kallmann's syndrome, constitutional delay, and hypothalamic hamartoma
Idiopathic, genetic, traumatic, iatrogenic, chemical, and radiation-related ovarian destruction
McCune-Albright syndrome, polycystic ovary syndrome, and hyperprolactinaemia
Craniopharyngioma, imperforate hymen, and androgen insensitivity syndrome

stem: CASE STUDY: A 17-year-old girl presents with cyclic lower abdominal pain that began 14 months ago. She has normal secondary sexual characteristics. Examination reveals a bulging bluish membrane at the vaginal introitus with no visible vaginal opening. Gonadotropins are within the normal range. This presentation is MOST consistent with which cause of delayed puberty?
options: Hypergonadotropic hypogonadism with primary streak gonadal failure causing outflow obstruction
Eugonadotrophic delayed puberty secondary to imperforate hymen causing cryptomenorrhoea
Hypogonadotropic hypogonadism secondary to craniopharyngioma causing anatomical outflow obstruction
Turner's syndrome with a functional but obstructed reproductive tract

stem: CASE STUDY: A 50-year-old menopausal woman on HRT for 6 months reports persistent irregular vaginal bleeding. Which absolute contraindication to HRT does this symptom POTENTIALLY represent, warranting immediate clinical investigation?
options: History of uterine fibroids classified as a relative contraindication that may now be exacerbated
Undiagnosed vaginal bleeding, which is an absolute contraindication if the cause has not been established
Venous thrombosis indicated by bleeding from thrombocytopenia as a HRT complication
Atypical ductal hyperplasia of the breast causing hormonal alteration of the uterine lining

stem: CASE STUDY: A 52-year-old woman on oral HRT wishes to switch to a non-oral route due to gastrointestinal side effects including nausea and bloating. Which non-oral alternatives are available according to the curriculum?
options: Intrauterine levonorgestrel device, vaginal ring, and nasal estrogen spray
Matrix and reservoir patches, gel, and intravaginal administration
Subcutaneous estradiol implant, rectal suppositories, and dermal aerosol spray
Intramuscular depot injection, sublingual troches, and buccal mucosal absorption tablets

stem: CASE STUDY: A 9-year-old girl presents with cafÃ©-au-lait spots on the skin, polyostotic fibrous dysplasia confirmed on X-ray, and breast development. Serum LH and FSH levels are LOW despite evident pubertal changes. Which condition does this triad MOST represent?
options: Gonadotropin-dependent central precocious puberty from a hypothalamic hamartoma
Gonadotropin-independent precocious puberty consistent with McCune-Albright syndrome
Langerhans cell histiocytosis involving the skin, bone, and hypothalamic-pituitary axis
Constitutional precocious puberty with an incidental cafÃ©-au-lait macule from neurofibromatosis

stem: CASE STUDY: A 6-year-old girl is brought to the paediatric gynaecology clinic with persistent vaginal discharge, vulvar erythema, and pruritis for 3 weeks. Her mother reports she was found putting a small toy in her underwear area. The MOST complete list of conditions that should be systematically excluded includes:
options: Candidal vaginitis only, as this is the most common cause in this age group
Poor hygiene, foreign body, sexually transmitted infection, and sexual abuse
Streptococcal vulvovaginitis and urinary tract infection as the two most likely concurrent diagnoses
Lichen sclerosus, pinworm infestation, and lactobacillus overgrowth from probiotic use

stem: CASE STUDY: For the same 6-year-old girl with vulvo-vaginitis (Q95), the nurse prepares for examination. Which examination positions are specifically recommended in the curriculum for this scenario?
options: Lithotomy position under general anaesthesia and left lateral Sims position
Knee-chest or frog-leg position
Supine with the legs extended flat on the examination table with a chaperone present
Dorsal recumbent with hip flexion of 90 degrees and a trained paediatric gynaecologist

stem: CASE STUDY: A 48-year-old woman with a history of multiple uterine fibroids and migraine headaches seeks HRT for menopausal symptoms. The nurse correctly informs her that both uterine fibroids and migraines are classified as which category of contraindication to HRT?
options: Absolute contraindications that permanently exclude her from all forms of HRT
Relative contraindications that do not automatically exclude HRT but require careful individual risk-benefit assessment
Minor adverse drug reactions that require dose adjustment but are not true contraindications
Absolute contraindications for oral HRT only, with non-oral routes remaining entirely safe

stem: CASE STUDY: A male patient who underwent orchidectomy at age 25 for testicular cancer now requests counselling regarding reproductive consequences. The nurse correctly advises that following castration after pubertal onset:
options: His libido will be completely unaffected because the hypothalamic testosterone set-point was established before the orchidectomy
His libido will typically be substantially reduced or eliminated in total and he is now permanently sterile
His libido will be maintained for at least 5 years because sufficient testosterone is stored in peripheral adipose tissue
He will remain fertile because spermatogenesis can be maintained by elevated LH levels stimulating any residual testicular tissue

stem: CASE STUDY: A researcher studying the history of human sexual physiology asks which specific pioneer researchers recorded the first laboratory data on the anatomy and physiology of human sexual response through direct observation between 1957 and 1965. The correct answer is:
options: Sigmund Freud and Carl Jung, who established the psychoanalytic framework for sexual response cycles
Masters and Johnson
Kinsey and Pomeroy, whose survey-based research first quantified human sexual behaviour
Havelock Ellis and Richard von Krafft-Ebing, whose clinical observations preceded laboratory research

stem: CASE STUDY: A nurse reviews a 45-year-old woman's notes prior to initiating HRT. The notes record: recent DVT 4 weeks ago, personal history of breast cancer treated 3 years ago, active cholangitis, migraine headaches, and a history of uterine fibroids. The nurse correctly identifies that the SINGLE finding which constitutes an ABSOLUTE contraindication mandating that HRT must NOT be initiated at this time is:
options: Personal history of breast cancer treated 3 years ago â a relative contraindication only
Recent DVT diagnosed 4 weeks ago â an absolute contraindication
Active cholangitis â classified as a relative rather than absolute contraindication
History of uterine fibroids â classified as a relative contraindication
